data.title: Stocks Slide On Trump Tariff Threats As Gold Hits Fresh Records
data.description:
Global equity markets retreated on Monday while gold and silver surged to new highs, after US President Donald Trump threatened additional tariffs on European goods linked to opposition over his Greenland plans.

Market sentiment turned cautious as Trump said the US would impose an extra 10% levy from February 1 on imports from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the Netherlands, Finland and Britain, with the rate set to rise to 25% from June 1 if no agreement on Greenland is reached. The move rattled investors already wary of escalating trade tensions between the US and Europe.

European markets bore the brunt of the sell-off. The STOXX 600 fell 1.2%, while major indices in Frankfurt, Paris and London declined between 0.4% and 1.7%. In Asia, Japan’s Nikkei dropped 0.7%, although MSCI’s broad Asia-Pacific index outside Japan was largely flat. US cash equity markets were closed for the Martin Luther King Jr. Day holiday, but S&P 500 and Nasdaq futures slid more than 1.2%.

“There is obviously a response (in financial markets) to the new tariff threats,” said George Lagarias, chief economist at Forvis Mazars. “It’s highly likely that the White House will use the threat of tariffs consistently, even when deals have previously been agreed.”

Safe-haven assets benefited as uncertainty grew. Gold climbed to a record high of US$4,689 an ounce, while silver jumped to US$94.08. Oil prices were little changed, with Brent easing 0.1% to US$64.04 a barrel as traders weighed trade war risks against easing unrest in Iran.

Currency markets saw an unusual reaction, with the US dollar weakening rather than strengthening. The euro rebounded 0.4% to US$1.1641, while the dollar slipped against the Swiss franc and yen. “Typically you see dollar strength in the wake of increased geopolitical risk but now we see dollar weakness as it is originating from the US,” said Handelsbanken FX strategist Tommy von Brömsen.

In China, markets were steady after data showed economic growth slowed to 4.5% in the December quarter, slightly above expectations. Strong industrial output offset weak retail sales, highlighting fragile domestic demand.

Attention is now turning to the World Economic Forum in Davos, where the tariff threats are expected to loom large as global leaders gather amid rising trade and geopolitical tensions.

Reuters

data.title: Ringgit Strengthens At Open As Dollar Slips On Greenland Tensions
data.description:
The ringgit extended its upward momentum to open firmer on Tuesday, tracking a weaker US Dollar Index (DXY) as tensions between the United States and the European Union escalated following renewed remarks by the administration of US President Donald Trump over Greenland.

At 8am, the local currency strengthened to 4.0475/0575 against the greenback, improving from Monday’s close of 4.0520/0570 as reported by Bernama.

Against major currencies, the ringgit traded mixed. It appreciated versus the Japanese yen to 2.5591/5656 from 2.5657/5692 previously, and edged higher against the British pound to 5.4313/4448 from 5.4321/4388.

However, it was marginally lower against the euro at 4.7109/7225 compared with 4.7100/7159 at the previous close.

The ringgit was mostly firmer against ASEAN currencies. It strengthened against the Singapore dollar to 3.1491/1571 from 3.1521/1562, rose versus the Thai baht to 12.9454/9886 from 12.9623/9845, and appreciated against the Indonesian rupiah to 238.7/239.4 from 239.0/239.3.

Meanwhile, the local note was little changed against the Philippine peso, trading at 6.81/6.83 compared with 6.81/6.82 on Monday.

data.title: SGX Opens In Red, Financials Lead Early Declines
data.description:
The Singapore Exchange (SGX) opened lower with the Straits Times Index (STI) down 24.50 points or 0.51% to 4,810.38 as of 9.09 am. Market activity was moderate, with 128.84 million shares traded worth S$115.16 million.

Of the active counters, 66 advanced while 91 declined, signalling early selling pressure. Key banks and blue chips were among the decliners, with DBS down 0.869% to S$58.20. OCBC Bank traded at S$20.25, UOB at S$36.64, Singtel at S$4.48, and SGX itself at S$17.44.

Elsewhere, Seatrium Ltd fell to S$2.14 while YZJ Shipbuilding was at S$3.50.

In derivatives trading, volume remained light with 20.64k contracts exchanged. SGX futures were mixed, with MSCI Singapore Index Futures at 451.75 and Nikkei 225 Index Futures at 53,185.

Other regional indices tracked by SGX included the iEdge Singapore Next 50 Index at 1,480.03, the iEdge S-REIT Index at 1,123.93, and the iEdge APAC Financials Dividend Plus Index at 3,613.61.

The early weakness on SGX reflects global risk-off sentiment, as investors digest developments in US-European trade tensions and ongoing market uncertainty across Asia.

data.title: STI Loses 0.7% As Traders Stay On Sidelines
data.description:
The Singapore Exchange (SGX) opened lower on Wednesday, with the Straits Times Index (STI) dropping 34.45 points, or 0.71%, to 4,793.55 amid cautious trading.

Early market activity saw 55 advancers and 142 decliners, with total volume reaching 98.08 million shares valued at S$145.85 million.

Among top movers, UOB traded at S$36.37, OCBC Bank at S$20.31, Singtel at S$4.55, YZJ Shipbuilding at S$3.51, CapitaLand Investment at S$2.93, and ST Engineering at S$9.53.

The iEdge Singapore Next 50 Index opened at 1,484.99, while the iEdge S-REIT Index stood at 1,133.02. The iEdge-OCBC Singapore Low Carbon Select 40 Capped Index was at 3,684.00, and the iEdge APAC Financials Dividend Plus Index opened at 3,584.63.

SGX derivatives also saw activity, with 12,530 contracts traded, including the Nikkei 225 Index Futures at 52,640.00 and the FTSE China A50 Index Futures at 15,067.00.

Traders remained cautious ahead of regional economic updates and global market developments, keeping early sentiment subdued.

data.title: Dollar Retreats As “Sell America” Trade Returns
data.description:
The US dollar retreated to its lowest level in a week on Monday, following renewed tariff threats by the White House towards the European Union over Greenland, triggering a broad selloff in US stocks and government bonds.

The dollar index, which tracks the greenback against a basket of six major currencies, fell 0.1% to 99.004, its lowest since Jan 14 as investors assessed risks of prolonged uncertainty, strained alliances, and potential retaliation.

“While there are hopes the U.S. administration may soon de-escalate these threats, as it has with prior tariff announcements, it is clear that securing Greenland remains a core national security objective for the current administration,” said Tony Sycamore, market analyst at IG in Sydney.

The yield on the U.S. 10-year Treasury bond rose 3 basis points to 4.2586%, while Fed funds futures implied a 94.5% probability that the Federal Reserve will keep rates on hold at its upcoming two-day meeting.

In currency markets, the dollar was flat at 158.175 yen following Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s announcement of snap elections for Feb 8. Against the offshore Chinese yuan, it held steady at 6.9536, while the euro and British pound were unchanged at US$1.1640 and US$1.3427 respectively.

Elsewhere, the Australian dollar slipped 0.1% to US$0.6710 and the New Zealand dollar edged down 0.1% to US$0.5794. In the crypto space, Bitcoin fell 0.6% to US$92,336.99, while Ether declined 1.1% to US$3,174.41.

Reuters

data.title: Ringgit Opens Firmer On Modest Demand Amid Cautious Trading
data.description:
The ringgit opened slightly stronger against the US dollar and other major currencies on Wednesday, supported by modest demand as traders remained cautious amid ongoing economic developments.

At 8 am, the local currency traded at 4.0525/0575 against the greenback, compared with Tuesday’s close of 4.0530/0580.

The ringgit also gained against a basket of major currencies. It rose against the Japanese yen to 2.5615/5648 from 2.5696/5729, edged up versus the British pound to 5.4462/4529 from 5.4618/4686, and appreciated against the euro to 4.7516/7574 from 4.7530/7588.

Against regional currencies, the local note was mostly firmer. It strengthened versus the Singapore dollar to 3.1557/1598 from 3.1602/1646 and against the Indonesian rupiah to 238.7/239.4 from 239.0/239.4.

However, the ringgit slipped slightly against the Thai baht to 13.0566/0824 from 13.0397/0629 and remained unchanged versus the Philippine peso at 6.81/6.83.

data.title: Bursa Opens In Negative Territory Amid Selective Sector Moves
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia opened lower on Tuesday, with the benchmark FBMKLCI falling 8.75 points, or 0.51%, to 1,703.58 as investors remained cautious.

The broader market mirrored the cautious tone. The FBM70 declined 86.18 points, or 0.49%, to 17,436.58, while the FTSE Bursa Malaysia Emas Index (FBMEMAS) dropped 54.91 points, or 0.44%, to 12,556.63. The FTSE Bursa Malaysia Shariah Index (FBMSHA) eased 49.89 points, or 0.41%, to 12,190.80.

Among early movers, Nestle (4707) led the top gainers, climbing 0.5 to RM116.50, followed by Allianz (1163), up 0.32 to RM22.00. MPI (3867) advanced 0.28 to RM34.00, while Infomedia (0265) and Tex Cycle (0089) rose 0.05 each.

On the losers’ board, Hong Leong Bank (5819) slipped 0.36 to RM23.94, followed by F&N (3689) down 0.34 to RM35.66, KLK (2445) off 0.16 to RM20.24, HLFG (1082) down 0.14 to RM21.44, and Heimbach (3255) easing 0.12 to RM22.88.

In terms of activity, TWL (7079) led the volume charts with 33.06 million shares traded, followed by SBS (0386) with 29.27 million. IJM (3336) fell 0.11 to RM2.54 on 13.07 million shares, while SMRT (0117) and Capital A (5099) each gained 0.015 to RM0.20 and RM0.52 respectively.

Market watchers note that early trading reflected selective buying in consumer and healthcare sectors, while finance and plantation stocks saw selling pressure.

data.title: Trump’s Greenland Tariff Threats Rattle Markets, Spur ‘Sell America’ Trades
data.description:
Asian stocks fell on Tuesday while the dollar remained under pressure and US Treasury yields climbed to their highest level in more than four months, as renewed trade-war concerns rattled risk sentiment.

Citi has downgraded European equities, while analysts said gold remained near record highs as investors sought safe-haven assets amid uncertainty over US-Europe trade relations.

The market turbulence follows US President Donald Trump’s threats to impose additional tariffs over Greenland, a move that risks intensifying trade tensions with Europe.

Futures for the Nasdaq and S&P 500 fell around 1% in early trade, while the yield on the 10-year US Treasury note rose to 4.265%, marking its highest level since early September.

The MSCI Asia-Pacific index outside Japan declined 0.44%, pulling further from last week’s record highs.

MUFG’s Europe economist Henry Cook said last year had shown not to overreact to Trump’s threats, but added that the latest remarks could keep tariff uncertainty elevated, even if de-escalation occurs.

Citi strategists noted that the heightened tensions weaken the near-term investment case for European equities and cast doubt on a broad-based earnings inflection in 2026. European futures opened 0.12% lower.

Investors are now eyeing the World Economic Forum in Davos, where Trump is expected to meet global business leaders on Wednesday.

Meanwhile, Japan’s Nikkei dropped 0.8% as the market anticipates the upcoming election, with Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi campaigning for tax cuts, higher spending, and a new security strategy.

Long- and short-term Japanese government bond yields surged on Monday amid concerns that tax cuts could worsen the country’s fiscal position.

Gold remained largely unchanged at US$4,670 per ounce, slightly below Monday’s record high, reflecting continued safe-haven demand.

The episode revives discussions of a “Sell America” trade, where investors offload US equities, the dollar, and Treasuries in response to policy uncertainties.

Analysts caution that any demand spike is likely short-lived, and structural supply pressures in key markets remain the main long-term concern.

Reuters

data.title: Bursa Stumbles Past 1,700 As SBS Leads Activity, Capital A Shines
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia edged lower at midday, with the benchmark FBM KLCI slipping 9.17 points or 0.54% to 1,703.16 as of 12.30 pm.

The broader market mirrored the retreat, with the FBM70 down 95.73 points (-0.55%) to 17,427.03 and the FBM Emas Index easing 64.90 points (-0.51%) to 12,546.64.

Trading activity was led by debutant SBS Bhd, which fell 1 sen to RM0.24 on volume of 66.1 million shares, followed by ZETRIX Bhd at RM0.805 with 45.3 million shares traded.

Capital A Bhd bucked the trend, rising 3 sen to RM0.535 on 39.7 million shares, while IJM Corp slipped 11 sen to RM2.54 with 38.9 million shares changing hands.

Among gainers, TimeCom Resources Bhd led the rally with a 15 sen gain to RM5.70, followed by Infomedia Asia Bhd (+12 sen to RM1.54), KLCC Bhd (+10 sen to RM9.11), LPI Capital Bhd (+10 sen to RM15.30) and Westports Holdings Bhd (+9 sen to RM6.00).

On the downside, Hong Leong Bank Bhd fell 66 sen to RM23.64, while Hong Leong Financial Group Bhd declined 66 sen to RM20.92.

Heineken Malaysia Bhd eased 50 sen to RM22.50, Nestlé (Malaysia) Bhd lost 30 sen to RM115.70 and UTD Plantations Bhd dropped 30 sen to RM30.90.

Market watchers noted the mixed momentum as investors weighed corporate earnings and ongoing global trade concerns, with selective buying in counters such as Capital A and LPI Capital supporting the market’s resilience.

data.title: Bursa Malaysia Closes Lower As FBM KLCI Dips 13.27 Points
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia ended Wednesday’s trading session on a softer note, with the FBM KLCI closing at 1,699.06 points, down 13.27 points or 0.77%.

Broader market indices also fell, with the FBM70 down 124.93 points (-0.71%), F4GBM slipping 8.35 points (-0.82%), and FBMEMAS retreating 96.34 points (-0.76%).

Market activity was led by high-volume counters including SBS, ZETRIX, and IJM.

SBS fell 1 sen to 24 sen, with 76.2 million shares changing hands, while ZETRIX held steady at 80.5 sen with 69.9 million shares traded.

IJM declined 8 sen to RM2.57 on 67.5 million shares.

On the gainers’ side, SMRT rose 0.5 sen to 19 sen and CAPITALA added 2 sen to 52.5 sen.

Overall, investors remained cautious amid mixed regional cues and profit-taking in selected heavyweight counters, contributing to the broad-based declines across the local bourse.

data.title: Asian Stocks Slide As ‘Sell America’ Fears And Greenland Tensions Weigh
data.description:
Asian equities extended losses for a third straight session on Wednesday as investor anxiety rose over US President Donald Trump’s threats to acquire Greenland and rekindled concerns of a global trade war.

Markets reacted to fears of offshore selling of US assets (dubbed the “Sell America” trade) after Wall Street tumbled over 2% overnight and the US dollar fell sharply, marking its largest decline in more than a month.

Precious metals surged as a result, with gold hitting a record high of US$4,806 an ounce, up 0.8%, and silver rising 0.4% to US$95.01 an ounce.

“The ‘sell America’ trade was the driving force behind major market moves overnight, as investors looked to reduce exposure to the US, seen by many as an unreliable partner pursuing self-defeating policies,” said Mantas Vanagas, senior economist at Westpac.

In early Asian trade, MSCI’s broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan slipped 0.3%, while Japan’s Nikkei 225 fell 1.2%, marking its fifth consecutive day of declines.

European futures were also lower, with both EURO STOXX 50 and DAX contracts down 0.4%.

The global bond market, already rattled by surging Japanese government bond yields, paused briefly.

Yields on 40-year Japanese government bonds fell 6 basis points to 4.145%, retreating from a record 4.215% on Tuesday.

US Treasury yields were largely steady, with the benchmark 10-year yield dipping 1 basis point to 4.285%.

The currency market remained volatile. The U.S. dollar steadied at 98.56 against major peers after its overnight drop of 0.5%, the largest daily fall since early December.

Meanwhile, the yen held at 158.19 per dollar, while the Swiss franc hit a record 200.19 yen.

Oil prices retreated as temporary production halts in Kazakhstan were outweighed by geopolitical tensions and an expected rise in US crude inventories.

West Texas Intermediate crude for March fell 1.31% to US$59.57 a barrel.

All eyes are now on Trump’s upcoming speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, where his remarks on Greenland and tariffs on Europe could further influence global markets.

Reuters

data.title: Gold, Silver Hit Records, And Stocks Fall As Trade Fear Returns
data.description:
Gold and silver hit record highs on Monday (Jan 19) while most equity markets fell after United States President Donald Trump revived trade war fears by threatening several European nations with tariffs over their opposition to the US buying Greenland.

Trump has fanned already-rising geopolitical tensions this month by insisting that Washington would take control of the North Atlantic island, citing national security needs.And on Saturday, after talks failed to resolve “fundamental disagreement” over the Danish autonomous territory, he announced he would hit eight countries with fresh levies over their refusal to submit to his demands.

He said he would impose 10 per cent tariffs on Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands and Finland from Feb 1 – rising to 25 per cent from Jun 1 – if they did not agree to the takeover.

The announcement drew an immediate response, with a joint statement from the countries saying: “Tariff threats undermine transatlantic relations and risk a dangerous downward spiral.”The move also threatened a trade deal signed between the US and European Union last year, with German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul telling ARD television: “I don’t believe that this agreement is possible in the current situation.”Meanwhile, aides to French President Emmanuel Macron said he would ask the EU to activate a never-before-used “anti-coercion instrument” against Washington if Trump makes good on his threat.

This measure allows for curbing imports of goods and services into the EU, a market of 27 countries with a combined population of 450 million.

data.title: European Stocks Fall, Dollar Drops, Markets Focus On Trump’s Tariff Threat
data.description:
European stocks fell in early trading on Tuesday, the dollar entered a second day of declines and U.S. Treasury yields hit a four-month high after U.S. President Donald Trump continued his push to take control of Greenland and threatened to reignite a trade war with Europe.

Trump said he no longer thought “purely of Peace” after he did not win the Nobel Peace Prize, and reiterated a threat to increase tariffs on EU members Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Sweden, and the Netherlands, along with Britain and Norway, until the U.S. is allowed to buy Greenland.

EU leaders will discuss options, including tariffs worth 93 billion euros ($109 billion) on U.S. imports, at an emergency summit on Brussels on Thursday.

Stock markets fell, extending Monday’s declines, as the threats from the U.S. reignited a debate over the “Sell America” trade that emerged after Trump’s “Liberation Day” levies announced last April.E

Europe’s STOXX 600 was down 1.4 per cent on the day at 0956 GMT, having fallen 1.2 per cent on Monday, while the MSCI World Equity Index was down 0.2 per cent. The FTSE 100 was down 1.4 per cent.Trump separately threatened to hit French wines and champagne with 200 per cent tariffs, in an apparent effort to cajole French President Emmanuel Macron to join his Board of Peace initiative.

Amelie Derambure, senior multi-asset portfolio manager at Amundi in Paris, said that the downward move in markets was “precautionary profit-taking and some risk reduction” but that markets were helped by the macroeconomic backdrop. Recent economic data has pointed to growth and decelerating inflation, and her portfolios remain risk-orientated, she said.

“The situation in Greenland (is) worrying the markets. For the moment it remains relatively contained, there is no panic,” she said.

“I don’t see for the moment anything comparable to Liberation Day or to something very dramatic for markets,” she added.

The dollar index was down 0.6 per cent on the day, in its second day of declines, at 98.485, and the euro was up 0.7 per cent against the dollar at $$1.1726, its highest since January 6.As investors weighed up the risk of Trump reigniting a trade war with Europe over Greenland, U.S. Treasury yields hit their highest since September in early trading.

U.S. markets were closed on Monday for a public holiday, so the moves were a delayed reaction to the developments that began over the weekend. 

The spread between the U.S. 30-year yield and the 10-year yield, as well as the spread between the two-year and 10-year yield, were both set for their biggest one-day steepening since August 2025.

Elsewhere, Japanese government bond yields hit record highs over concerns that tax cuts will make the government’s finances worse. Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi had called a snap general election on Monday.

Oil prices edged higher, with Brent crude futures up 0.2 per cent at $64.01 a barrel and U.S. West Texas Intermediate up 0.5 per cent at $59.72 a barrel, as prices were supported by expectations for global economic growth. 

Reuters

data.title: Bursa Dips As Profit-Taking Weighs On Key Indexes
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia’s key indices were slightly lower at midday, reflecting cautious investor sentiment amid profit-taking.

The benchmark FBM KLCI slipped 2.36 points, or 0.14%, to 1,710.38 by 12:35 PM. Broader market indexes followed suit, with the FBM70 down 108.30 points, or 0.61%, to 17,577.31, while the FBM Emas Index eased 32.66 points, or 0.26%, to 12,612.37.

On the losers’ board, SMRT Holdings was among the heaviest decliners, falling 0.25 to RM0.195, with a trading volume of over 2 billion shares, following an earlier downgrade by HLIB Research.

Other notable decliners included United Plantations, which fell 0.68 to RM31.02, and Malaysian Pacific Industries, down 0.38 to RM34.10.

Among gainers, Allianz rose 1.06 to RM21.50 on active buying interest, while Hong Leong Financial Group gained 0.60 to RM21.56. HL Bank, HL Insurance and LPI also contributed to the positive momentum, recording moderate gains by midday.

The top traded stocks by volume were dominated by SMRT Holdings, CapitalA and AAX, reflecting continued investor focus on high-liquidity counters despite the overall market dip.

Market participants cited selective profit-taking and sector rotation as key drivers behind the cautious session, with investors closely watching upcoming corporate earnings and broader economic indicators for further direction.

data.title: Tariff Jitters Send Wall Street To Worst Day In Three Months
data.description:
US stocks suffered their steepest one-day losses in three months on Tuesday as investors reacted to renewed tariff threats from President Donald Trump, triggering a broad risk-off move across global markets.

All three major Wall Street indexes recorded their worst daily performance since October 10, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite both slipping below their 50-day moving averages.

The S&P 500 fell 143.15 points, or 2.06%, to close at 6,796.86, while the Nasdaq slid 561.07 points, or 2.39%, to 22,954.32.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 870.74 points, or 1.76%, to 48,488.59.

The selloff coincided with Trump’s weekend remarks, which marked the first opportunity for investors to respond following the Martin Luther King Jr. Day market holiday.

Trump said an additional 10% import tariff would take effect on February 1 on goods from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the Netherlands, Finland and Great Britain, countries already subject to US tariffs.

He later said the tariffs would rise to 25% from June 1 and remain in place until an agreement was reached for the US to purchase Greenland, a proposal rejected by leaders in Greenland and Denmark, who insisted the island was not for sale.

The return of tariff rhetoric revived memories of April’s “Liberation Day”, when sweeping levies on global trading partners pushed the S&P 500 close to bear market territory.

Market volatility surged, with the CBOE Volatility Index closing at 20.09, its highest level since November 24.

Risk aversion rippled across asset classes. Gold surged to fresh record highs, US Treasury yields rose amid renewed selling pressure, and bitcoin fell more than 3%.

Trading volumes also climbed, with about 20.6 billion shares changing hands, well above the 20-day average of 17.01 billion.

Despite the sharp moves, some market participants questioned whether the reaction would have lasting consequences. Jamie Cox, managing partner at Harris Financial Group, said he did not see signs of a sustained investor exit.

“I’m not at the point yet where I’m willing to say what is happening with Greenland, and the resurgence of the tariff threat back ⁠and forth, is going to precipitate a ⁠correction in the equities markets,” he said, adding that he would be surprised to see a 3% to 5% market drop this week.

Bond markets also came under pressure globally. Japanese government bonds plunged, sending yields to record highs after Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s call for a snap election raised concerns over fiscal stability.

The selloff spilled into Europe, pushing up long-term bond yields, while losses in US Treasuries were most pronounced at the long end of the curve.

Looking ahead, investors are set to digest a slate of economic data this week, including third-quarter US GDP figures, January PMI readings and the Personal Consumption Expenditures report, the Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation gauge.

Corporate earnings season is also gaining momentum, with several major companies due to report, including Netflix, which closed 0.8% lower ahead of its earnings announcement.

Reuters

data.title: Financials And Energy Lead Gains While Construction And Tech Drag
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia closed lower today, with gains led by heavyweight financial and energy stocks, while losses in construction, technology and selected property counters capped overall sentiment.

Among the top gainers, Allianz Malaysia Bhd rose RM1.24 to RM21.68, with over 3.2 million shares traded, as interest returned to defensive insurance plays. Hong Leong Financial Group Bhd added RM0.62 to RM21.58, supported by steady demand in banking counters amid expectations of resilient earnings.

Energy related stocks also featured prominently, with Petronas Dagangan Bhd climbing RM0.42 to RM21.08, while plantation heavyweight Kuala Lumpur Kepong Bhd gained RM0.40 to RM20.40. Hong Leong Bank Bhd completed the top gainers list, rising RM0.36 to RM24.30 on strong volume.

On the downside, MPI Bhd led the decliners, sliding RM0.76 to RM33.72, reflecting continued weakness in the technology space. United Plantations Bhd fell RM0.50 to RM31.20, while Smart Asia Chemical Bhd saw heavy selling pressure, dropping RM0.26 to 18.5 sen on exceptionally high volume.

Property and construction stocks also weighed on the market, with KLCC Stapled Group easing RM0.23 to RM9.01 and IJM Corp Bhd slipping RM0.15 to RM2.65, as investors remained cautious on sector near term prospects.

Overall, market activity suggested selective buying in defensives and high quality large caps, while profit taking continued in cyclical and growth oriented stocks.

data.title: Bursa Malaysia Ends Lower As Broader Market Weakens Despite Flat FBM KLCI
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia closed lower as selling pressure across mid cap and small cap stocks weighed on overall market sentiment, despite the benchmark FBM KLCI finishing largely unchanged.

The FBM KLCI edged down 0.41 points, or 0.02%, to close at 1,712.33 after moving between an intraday high of 1,712.33 and a low of 1,704.64. Blue chip stocks were mixed, helping to limit losses at the headline index level.

However, broader market indices saw steeper declines. The FBM 70 slid 162.85 points, or 0.92%, to 17,522.76, while the FBM EMAS Index fell 33.49 points to 12,611.54. The FBM Small Cap Index dropped 62.13 points, or 0.51%, reflecting continued risk off sentiment among retail and short term traders.

The FBM 4G Benchmark Index also ended lower, down 2.50 points to 1,015.73.

Trading activity was dominated by heavily traded penny and recovery stocks. Smart Asia Chemical Bhd emerged as the most active counter, falling 26 sen to 18.5 sen after swinging between 33 sen and 15.5 sen, with more than 2.39 billion shares changing hands.

Capital A Bhd declined four sen to 50.5 sen, while IJM Corp Bhd fell 15 sen to RM2.65 amid persistent selling in construction related counters. Zetrix AI Bhd eased one sen to 80.5 sen, while AirAsia X Bhd slipped two sen to RM1.65.

Overall, market sentiment remained cautious as investors rotated away from smaller capitalisation stocks, with defensive buying in select heavyweights helping to cushion losses at the benchmark level.
